signing.simpleSignatureNoticeText: This is a simple electronic signature (typed name). It is enforceable for most contracts under the U.S. ESIGN Act and EU eIDAS Regulation. Some legal acts (notarial deeds, certain real-estate transfers) require a qualified electronic signature, which this product does not provide.

landing.headline: Launch your Delaware C-Corp in an afternoon.
landing.subheadline: Pick a few business choices in plain English. We generate your Certificate of Incorporation, Founders' Agreements, and IP Assignments — ready to file with Delaware and share with a lawyer.
landing.card1Body: One company profile flows into every document. You enter the company name and your founders once.
landing.card2Body: "Should a founder keep their shares if they leave in year one?" — you answer; we translate.
landing.card3Body: Hand any step to a vetted attorney from our directory for review — or keep going on your own.
landing.ctaPanelBody: About 8 minutes for the Foundation step — longer if you want to think through each question.
landing.pickUp: Pick up where you left off

step.willCreateOther: We'll create {count} documents for {company}. Each one will be pre-filled from your company profile and your answers to the questions above.

step.registeredAgentHint: You can also fill this in later when you're ready to file with Delaware.

vesting.question: If a founder leaves in the first year, should they keep the shares they haven't earned?
vesting.advancedDisclosure: Translates to vesting schedule and cliff in the Founders' Agreement. 'Standard' = four-year vesting with a one-year cliff, the default expectation of most VCs.
vesting.standardLabel: No — vest over 4 years with a 1-year cliff
vesting.standardDescription: Founders earn their shares over four years; leaving in year one means they walk away with none. Standard for venture-backed startups.
vesting.founderFriendlyDescription: Faster vesting, no all-or-nothing first year. Some early-stage teams prefer this when co-founders have an established relationship.
vesting.noneLabel: No vesting — they keep whatever they're issued
vesting.noneDescription: Riskiest option. If a founder leaves on day two, the cap table stays with them. Hard to raise a priced round later.

ipScope.question: Should founders assign every invention — past and future — that relates to the company?
ipScope.advancedDisclosure: Translates to the scope clause in each founder's IP Assignment. 'Broad' captures prior inventions relating to the business; 'narrow' only captures inventions made on company time.
ipScope.broadLabel: Yes — assign everything relevant to the company, past and future
ipScope.broadDescription: Standard for investor-friendly startups. Protects the company's IP foundation.
ipScope.narrowLabel: Only inventions made during company work
ipScope.narrowDescription: Less common; may leave ambiguity over inventions created before formation.

foundation.title: You've already formed your company
foundation.explainer: We won't generate anything for this step — marking it as done just unlocks the later steps so you can keep moving.

hiring.title: You've already hired your team
hiring.explainer: We'll skip the Hiring step. If you add more hires later, you can always come back and generate fresh offer letters or option grants.

hiring.doc2: Signed IP assignment or PIIA from each employee

lawyerWarning.riskWarning: Negotiating without legal counsel may expose you to unfavorable terms. While the platform guides you through options and their implications, it does not replace professional legal advice.

lawyerWarning.stageBDescription: Once all clauses are agreed, the initiator can request a shared closing attorney to help both parties finalize.

marketplace.title: Skill Marketplace
marketplace.subtitle: Browse and enable premium contract skills

a2aSkills.title: Agent-to-Agent Contract Skills
a2aSkills.subtitle: Browse the contract types available for autonomous agent negotiation. Each skill represents a legally-grounded contract that your agents can negotiate on behalf of your organization.
a2aSkills.emptyState: No A2A contract skills are currently available. Contact your administrator.
a2aSkills.gavelRecommended: Recommended for agent-to-agent disputes — fast, electronic, binding
a2aSkills.howItWorksDesc: Your organization configures a playbook with preferred positions on each clause. When your agent encounters a counterparty, both sides submit their playbooks and Dealroom's compromise algorithm finds the optimal agreement — or identifies irreconcilable positions.
a2aSkills.step1Desc: Browse the skills below to understand what terms your agent will negotiate.
a2aSkills.step2Desc: For each clause, choose your preferred option, set flexibility (1-5), and mark red lines.
a2aSkills.step3Desc: Your agent uses the playbook via the API. Agreed contracts are generated as binding PDFs.
a2aSkills.catalogDesc: Click any contract type to see its negotiable clauses and options. Each option shows which party it favors, so you can make informed choices when configuring your playbook.

agentPrep.title: Agent Preparation Guide
agentPrep.subtitle: Your agent will negotiate binding contracts on behalf of your organization. Preparation is the difference between legal certainty and unwanted exposure.
agentPrep.heroHeading: Before Your Agent Signs a Contract
agentPrep.heroDesc: When an AI agent negotiates a contract through Dealroom, the result is a legally binding agreement between the organizations behind each agent. The agent acts as your representative — its decisions become your obligations. Preparation ensures those decisions reflect your actual business needs.
agentPrep.artifactsDesc: Effective agent preparation produces three complementary outputs:
agentPrep.artifactPolicyDesc: A human-readable strategy document — like a CLAUDE.md for your agent — that encodes your organization's risk tolerance, priorities, and red lines in natural language.
agentPrep.artifactPlaybookDesc: The machine-readable JSON configuration submitted via the API. Translates your policy into specific clause preferences, flexibility scores, and non-negotiable positions.
agentPrep.artifactDrcDesc: A Gavel DRC that defines how disputes are resolved if the contract is breached — resolution tiers, escrow requirements, and precedent preferences.
agentPrep.whyDesc: The compromise algorithm gives more weight to the party with higher stakes on each clause. Preparation lets you control where your stakes are highest.
agentPrep.withoutDesc: Your agent uses default flexibility on every clause. The algorithm treats all positions as equally important. You may accept terms your organization would never agree to — on liability caps, data handling, or IP ownership.
agentPrep.withDesc: Your agent knows which clauses matter most. Low flexibility on critical terms means higher stakes, giving your position more weight. Red lines prevent the algorithm from compromising on non-negotiable positions. Satisfaction scores rise from ~60% to ~85%+.
agentPrep.withDrcDesc: If the agreed contract is later breached, disputes resolve in hours or days through Gavel's automated arbitration — not months in court. Escrow secures potential recovery. Your organization is protected end-to-end.
agentPrep.flowStep1Desc: Review available contract types and identify which clauses matter to your business.
agentPrep.flowStep2Desc: Document your organization's priorities, risk tolerance, and non-negotiable positions.
agentPrep.flowStep3Desc: Translate your policy into API-ready playbook entries with flexibility scores and red lines.
agentPrep.flowStep4Desc: Choose your dispute resolution tiers, escrow parameters, and precedent preferences.
agentPrep.flowStep5Desc: Your agent negotiates with clear boundaries. Contracts reflect your actual business intent.
agentPrep.nextPolicyDesc: Start with the strategy document that guides all downstream decisions.
agentPrep.nextPlaybookDesc: Translate policy into machine-readable negotiation preferences.
agentPrep.nextDisputesDesc: Configure Gavel dispute resolution as your safety net.

agentPrepPolicy.title: Writing Your Agent Policy
agentPrepPolicy.subtitle: The Agent Policy is a natural-language strategy document that tells your agent (or its developer) how to approach contract negotiations on behalf of your organization.
agentPrepPolicy.whatIsDesc: An Agent Policy is a document — written in plain language, not code — that captures your organization's negotiation strategy. Think of it like a brief to a lawyer: it explains who you are, what matters to you, and where your limits are. Your agent or its developer uses this document as context when configuring the playbook that the API consumes.
agentPrepPolicy.whatIsNotDesc: The policy is not submitted to the Dealroom API directly. It is the input to the human or AI process that creates the playbook. It can be a markdown file, an internal wiki page, or a PDF — whatever your organization uses for policy documents.
agentPrepPolicy.templateDesc: Use this template as a starting point. Adapt the sections to your organization's needs.
agentPrepPolicy.templateIdentityDesc: Who you are — industry, size, jurisdiction, key regulatory constraints. This context helps calibrate risk tolerance.
agentPrepPolicy.templateRiskDesc: Your overall posture: conservative (protect at all costs), moderate (balanced), or aggressive (prioritize speed and flexibility). This sets the default flexibility level for clauses not explicitly addressed.
agentPrepPolicy.templatePrioritiesDesc: For each clause category (data handling, liability, IP, SLAs, termination, dispute resolution), state your preferred position and how important it is. Reference the A2A Skills Catalog to see available options.
agentPrepPolicy.templateRedLinesDesc: Absolute limits that your agent must never cross. Include the business reasoning — this helps the developer set acceptableOptions correctly. If both parties have irreconcilable red lines, the deal fails rather than producing an unacceptable contract.
agentPrepPolicy.templateDisputeDesc: Your preferred Gavel configuration: which resolution tiers to use, maximum escrow you are willing to post, whether to opt into the precedent database.
agentPrepPolicy.templateEscalationDesc: When should the agent pause and involve a human? Define thresholds: overall satisfaction below X%, specific clause categories, contract values above Y.
agentPrepPolicy.examplesDesc: Two contrasting approaches to illustrate how the same template produces very different negotiation strategies.
agentPrepPolicy.exampleAggressiveProfile: Aggressive — fast-moving, values speed over protection
agentPrepPolicy.mistake1Title: Setting everything as a red line
agentPrepPolicy.mistake1Desc: If every clause is non-negotiable, most negotiations will fail. Reserve red lines for genuinely critical positions — 2-3 per contract type is typical.
agentPrepPolicy.mistake2Desc: A perfect contract is worthless if you cannot enforce it. Configuring Gavel DRC parameters takes minutes and saves months if a dispute arises.
agentPrepPolicy.mistake3Desc: "We want good terms" is not a policy. Specify which clause options you prefer and why. Your developer cannot build a playbook from abstract intentions.
agentPrepPolicy.mistake4Desc: Each contract type has specific clauses with specific options. Review the A2A Skills Catalog before writing your policy — you cannot set preferences for clauses you have not seen.
agentPrepPolicy.nextStepDesc: Once your policy is written, translate it into a machine-readable playbook.

agentPrepPlaybook.title: Building Your Playbook
agentPrepPlaybook.subtitle: A playbook translates your Agent Policy into the machine-readable format that the Dealroom API consumes. Each entry maps a clause to your preferred option, flexibility level, and red line status.
agentPrepPlaybook.translationDesc: Each statement in your Agent Policy maps to a specific playbook entry. The policy says what you want in human terms; the playbook says it in API terms.
agentPrepPlaybook.translationExample1Policy: "Data must be deleted within 30 days of termination — non-negotiable"
agentPrepPlaybook.flexibilityDesc: Flexibility (1-5) is the most important setting in your playbook. It directly controls how much weight the compromise algorithm gives to your position on each clause.
agentPrepPlaybook.flexibilityFormulaDesc: stake = ((5 - flexibility) / 5 x 0.6) + (|bias| x 0.4). The party with the higher stake on a clause gets more influence in the compromise.
agentPrepPlaybook.flexibility1Desc: Stake contribution: 0.48. Your position carries maximum weight. Use for critical clauses.
agentPrepPlaybook.flexibility2Desc: Stake contribution: 0.36. Strong preference. The algorithm will compromise toward your position.
agentPrepPlaybook.flexibility3Desc: Stake contribution: 0.24. Balanced. Outcome depends on the counterparty's flexibility.
agentPrepPlaybook.flexibility4Desc: Stake contribution: 0.12. You signal willingness to accept the other side's preference.
agentPrepPlaybook.flexibility5Desc: Stake contribution: 0.00. You effectively defer to the counterparty on this clause.
agentPrepPlaybook.redLinesDesc: A red line tells the algorithm: this position is non-negotiable. If both parties have irreconcilable red lines on the same clause, the deal fails — which is the correct outcome when fundamental disagreement exists.
agentPrepPlaybook.redLineWhen1: Regulatory requirements (e.g., GDPR data deletion obligations)
agentPrepPlaybook.redLineWhen2: Board-mandated limits (e.g., liability caps, governing law)
agentPrepPlaybook.redLineWhen3: Operational constraints (e.g., minimum SLA for critical infrastructure)
agentPrepPlaybook.redLineAcceptableDesc: When isRedLine is true, use acceptableOptions to define the range of outcomes you can live with. If left empty, only your preferredOptionId is acceptable.
agentPrepPlaybook.explorerDesc: Select a contract type to see its clauses and build playbook entries interactively.
agentPrepPlaybook.fullExampleDesc: A full playbook for the API Access Agreement contract type, based on a moderate risk profile:
agentPrepPlaybook.checklistDesc: Before submitting your playbook via the API, verify:
agentPrepPlaybook.check1: Every required clause in the template has an entry
agentPrepPlaybook.check2: All clauseId values match the template exactly
agentPrepPlaybook.check3: All preferredOptionId values are valid option codes
agentPrepPlaybook.check4: All acceptableOptions values are valid option codes
agentPrepPlaybook.check5: Red lines are used sparingly (2-3 per contract type)
agentPrepPlaybook.check6: Flexibility values reflect your actual priorities, not defaults

agentPrepDisputes.title: Preparing for Disputes
agentPrepDisputes.subtitle: Contracts negotiated at machine speed can be breached at machine speed. A pre-configured dispute resolution path ensures your organization can enforce its agreements without months of litigation.
agentPrepDisputes.whyDesc: When your agent negotiates a contract that includes Gavel automated arbitration as the dispute resolution mechanism, you need to know what that means in practice — before a dispute arises, not after.
agentPrepDisputes.gavelDesc: Gavel is a 4-tier digital arbitration protocol designed for agent-to-agent commerce. Each tier escalates in formality, cost, and time — giving parties the opportunity to resolve disputes at the lowest appropriate level.
agentPrepDisputes.tier1Desc: Both parties' positions are submitted electronically. The system attempts to find a resolution using structured negotiation — similar to how Dealroom's compromise algorithm works, but applied to the dispute itself.
agentPrepDisputes.tier2Desc: An AI mediator analyzes both positions, the contract terms, and relevant precedent. It proposes a non-binding settlement. Parties can accept, reject, or counter-propose.
agentPrepDisputes.tier3Desc: An AI arbitrator reviews all evidence and renders a binding decision. Optional Neutral Counsel reviews the proceeding for procedural fairness. The award is enforceable under the New York Convention.
agentPrepDisputes.tier4Desc: A human arbitration panel reviews the case. This is the fallback for high-value or complex disputes where parties want human judgment. Neutral Counsel is mandatory.
agentPrepDisputes.drcDesc: A DRC is a machine-readable JSON object that defines how disputes under a contract will be resolved. When your agent selects Gavel arbitration in a Dealroom contract, these parameters govern the dispute process.
agentPrepDisputes.drcFieldParties: Identifies each party — agents reference their human/org principal via the principal field
agentPrepDisputes.drcFieldDispute: Governing law, seat of arbitration, language, and maximum claim value in stablecoin
agentPrepDisputes.drcFieldTiers: Which resolution tiers to use and their time limits — you can include 1-4 tiers
agentPrepDisputes.drcFieldEscrow: Blockchain escrow: chain (Base or Ethereum), token (USDC or DAI), percentage each party must stake
agentPrepDisputes.drcFieldPrecedent: Whether to publish anonymized awards and query prior decisions for similar cases
agentPrepDisputes.configDesc: Choose your DRC parameters based on your organization's risk profile and typical contract values.
agentPrepDisputes.configTiersDesc: Micro-transactions ($100-$1,000): 2 tiers (automated + AI mediation). Standard ($1,000-$50,000): 3-4 tiers. Enterprise ($50,000+): all 4 tiers with Neutral Counsel.
agentPrepDisputes.configEscrowDesc: The stake each party deposits. Higher stakes discourage frivolous disputes but require more liquidity. Typical range: 2-10% of the maximum claim value.
agentPrepDisputes.configChainDesc: Base L2: lower gas fees, faster settlement, suitable for most disputes. Ethereum: higher security and decentralization, suitable for very high-value disputes.
agentPrepDisputes.configPrecedentDesc: Publishing anonymized awards helps the ecosystem develop consistent standards. Querying precedent gives the AI arbitrator better context. Both are optional but recommended.
agentPrepDisputes.templatesDesc: Start with one of these templates and adjust to your needs.
agentPrepDisputes.templateMicroDesc: For x402 payments and small API transactions. Fast resolution, minimal escrow.
agentPrepDisputes.templateStandardDesc: For typical A2A contract negotiations. Full 4-tier resolution with moderate escrow.
agentPrepDisputes.templateEnterpriseDesc: For high-value contracts requiring maximum procedural safeguards.
agentPrepDisputes.connectionDesc: When your agent negotiates a contract on Dealroom and selects the Gavel Automated Arbitration option in the dispute-resolution clause, the DRC parameters you have configured define the resolution procedure. The DRC is embedded in the agreed contract, making it enforceable.
agentPrepDisputes.escalationDesc: If a contract is breached, your agent (or your team) can escalate to Gavel via the API:
agentPrepDisputes.escalationNote: Only FAILED or AGREED deals can be disputed. The Gavel case is created automatically with the DRC parameters from the contract.

localDeployment.title: Local Deployment
localDeployment.subtitle: Run Dealroom on your own infrastructure with full control over your data. Browse the Skills Marketplace, download signed skill packages, and install them locally via CLI.
localDeployment.howItWorksDesc: Self-hosted deployments follow a simple flow to acquire and activate premium skills:
localDeployment.step1Desc: The Skills Marketplace lists all available contract templates. Each skill shows its supported jurisdictions, languages, and clause count.
localDeployment.jurisdictionFiltersDesc: Filter by governing law — California, England & Wales, or Spain — to see skills tailored to your jurisdiction.
localDeployment.skillCardsDesc: Each card displays the skill name, description, clause count, and your current entitlement status (Active, Free, or price).
localDeployment.step2Desc: Click "Enable" on any premium skill to start a Stripe checkout. Once payment completes, an entitlement is created automatically.
localDeployment.secureCheckoutDesc: Stripe-powered payment. Your card is never stored on our servers.
localDeployment.instantEntitlementDesc: Webhook confirms payment and grants access to the skill within seconds.
localDeployment.downloadReadyDesc: After purchase you'll receive a download link by email and on your billing page.
localDeployment.step3Desc: Download the .skill file from your billing page or the link in your confirmation email. Each package is a signed ZIP archive.
localDeployment.tamperProofDesc: Every .skill file is signed with an Ed25519 key. The installer verifies the signature and file hashes before installing — any modification to the package will be detected and rejected.
localDeployment.step4Desc: Use the Dealroom CLI to verify and install the downloaded package into your local database.
localDeployment.step5Desc: Premium skills require license activation tied to your machine. This prevents unauthorized redistribution while giving you full offline access.
localDeployment.machineFingerprintDesc: A unique identifier derived from your hardware. This fingerprint is sent to the licensing server when you activate.
localDeployment.activateLicenseDesc: Use the license key from your purchase confirmation to activate premium features on this machine.
localDeployment.stateActiveDesc: Full access to all skill features including document generation
localDeployment.stateTasterDesc: Preview mode — negotiate but no final document export
localDeployment.stateExpiredDesc: Subscription lapsed — renew to restore access
localDeployment.cliReferenceDesc: All skill management commands available in the Dealroom CLI:
localDeployment.cmdSkillList: List all installed skills and their status
localDeployment.cmdSkillVerify: Verify a .skill package signature and integrity
localDeployment.cmdSkillInstall: Install a .skill package to the local database
localDeployment.cmdSkillActivate: Activate a premium license on this machine
localDeployment.cmdLicenseStatus: Check license status for all installed skills
localDeployment.troubleSignatureDesc: The .skill file may have been corrupted during download. Re-download from the billing page and try again. If the error persists, check that your Dealroom version matches the package's requirements.
localDeployment.troubleEntitlementDesc: Ensure you are logged in with the same account used for purchase. Download links from email expire after 7 days — use the billing page for a fresh link.
localDeployment.troubleActivationDesc: Each license allows a limited number of machine activations. Deactivate unused machines from your billing page, or contact support to increase your activation limit.

docsHome.heroSubtitle: Learn how asynchronous, algorithm-driven contract negotiation works — from deal creation through to signing.
docsHome.propAsyncDesc: Each party submits preferences independently. No scheduling, no back-and-forth calls.
docsHome.propAlgorithmDesc: A mathematical algorithm resolves differences based on each party's firmness and bias scores.
docsHome.propAttorneyDesc: Each party can independently involve legal counsel at any stage of the process.
docsHome.sectionLifecycleDesc: The complete deal lifecycle from creation to signing, including all six deal statuses.
docsHome.sectionCompromiseDesc: How the weighted stake formula resolves disagreements fairly for both parties.
docsHome.sectionSkillsDesc: Contract template packages, clause structures, taster vs. premium tiers, and multilingual support.
docsHome.sectionSupervisionDesc: Platform administration, supervisor roles, assignment-based access, and audit trails.
docsHome.sectionAgentPrepDesc: Prepare your organization for agent-negotiated contracts: write policies, build playbooks, and configure dispute resolution.
docsHome.quickStartIntro: New to Dealroom? Here's how a typical negotiation works:
docsHome.step1Desc: Choose a contract type, jurisdiction, and language.
docsHome.step2Desc: Send an email invitation to the other party.
docsHome.step3Desc: Each side independently chooses clause options and sets how firm they are on each.
docsHome.step4Desc: The weighted compromise engine resolves differences automatically.
docsHome.step5Desc: Accept the compromise or counter-propose on specific clauses.
docsHome.step6Desc: Both parties sign electronically once all clauses are agreed.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.subtitle: Dealroom manages the complete lifecycle of a two-party contract negotiation — from deal creation through electronic signing.
howItWorks.lifecycleIntro: Every deal progresses through six statuses. The platform handles transitions automatically as both parties complete their actions.
howItWorks.ws1Desc: The initiating party creates a new deal by selecting three parameters:
howItWorks.ws1ContractTypeDesc: NDA, SaaS, DPA, MSA, or premium templates from the marketplace.
howItWorks.ws1JurisdictionDesc: California, England & Wales, or Spain — determines available clauses.
howItWorks.ws1DealNameDesc: A descriptive name for internal tracking (e.g. 'Acme NDA Q2').
howItWorks.ws2Desc: The initiator sends an email invitation to the other party to join the negotiation.
howItWorks.ws2Detail: The counterparty receives a magic link by email. No account creation required — they authenticate via email and are automatically joined to the deal.
howItWorks.ws3Desc: Both parties independently choose their preferred position for each negotiable clause.
howItWorks.ws3OptionDesc: The clause variant that best represents this party's interests.
howItWorks.ws3PriorityDesc: A single slider indicating how strongly the party feels about this clause. Higher firmness = stronger negotiating stance.
howItWorks.ws3OpenFieldsDesc: Negotiable parameters (amounts, dates, scope) that either party can propose changes to during review.
howItWorks.ws3BlindNote: Clause selections and firmness are blind — neither party can see what the other has chosen until both have submitted. Open fields can be negotiated during the review phase.
howItWorks.ws4Desc: Once both parties submit, the algorithm calculates a fair compromise for each clause.
howItWorks.ws4FormulaNote: The party with the higher stake wins the clause. <link>Learn more about the algorithm →</link>
howItWorks.ws5Desc: Each party reviews the compromise proposal and decides how to respond.
howItWorks.ws5AcceptDesc: Agree with the proposed compromise for this clause.
howItWorks.ws5CounterDesc: Propose an alternative option and re-enter negotiation on this clause.
howItWorks.ws5CounterNote: Counter-proposals trigger a new round of compromise calculation. Negotiations continue until all clauses are agreed or a party withdraws.
howItWorks.ws6Desc: Once all clauses are agreed, both parties sign the final contract electronically.
howItWorks.conceptAsyncDesc: Parties don't need to be online at the same time. Each side submits preferences on their own schedule, and the system resolves differences when both are ready.
howItWorks.conceptBlindDesc: Neither party sees the other's selections until both have submitted. This prevents anchoring bias and ensures genuine position-taking rather than reactive bargaining.
howItWorks.conceptWeightedDesc: The algorithm doesn't split the difference — it awards each clause to the party with a higher calculated stake based on firmness and the option's inherent bias.
howItWorks.conceptJurisdictionDesc: Each contract template includes jurisdiction-specific provisions. Some clause options are only available under certain governing laws (e.g. Spain-specific termination compensation).

compromise.title: Compromise Algorithm
compromise.subtitle: How Dealroom resolves disagreements fairly using a weighted stake formula.
compromise.problemDesc: Contract negotiations typically suffer from three structural problems:
compromise.problemPowerImbalanceDesc: The party with more leverage dictates terms regardless of actual needs.
compromise.problemEndlessCyclesDesc: Contracts bounce back and forth as each side redlines the other's edits.
compromise.problemArbitrarySplitsDesc: When parties do compromise, it's often a 50/50 split that satisfies neither side.
compromise.solutionDesc: Dealroom replaces adversarial bargaining with a mathematical model that measures how much each clause matters to each party.
compromise.stakeInflexibilityDesc: How strongly the party feels about this clause (1–5 scale). Higher firmness = less willing to accept alternatives.
compromise.stakeBiasDesc: How much the chosen option inherently favors one party over the other.
compromise.fairOutcome1: The party who cares more about a clause is more likely to get their preferred option.
compromise.fairOutcome2: Openness is rewarded — rigid parties pay a higher negotiation cost.
compromise.fairOutcome3: Extreme positions (high bias) increase a party's stake, especially when combined with high firmness.
compromise.fairOutcome4: Global fairness rebalancing ensures no party wins every clause.
compromise.formulaInflexibilityDesc: Higher firmness (lower flexibility) increases the stake. A party at maximum firmness adds 60% to their stake score.
compromise.formulaBiasDesc: The absolute bias of the chosen option. Extreme options (±0.5) add more stake than balanced ones (0.0).
compromise.formulaResultDesc: The final stake value ranges from 0.0 to 1.0. The party with the higher stake for a clause wins that clause.
compromise.decisionDesc: For each clause, the algorithm compares both parties' stakes and makes a decision:
compromise.decisionSimilarDesc: When both parties have similar stakes (within 10%), the algorithm selects the most balanced option — the one with bias closest to 0.0.
compromise.decisionHigherDesc: When one party has a clearly higher stake, the algorithm checks the loser's flexibility to decide:
compromise.decisionFlexibleDesc: Winner gets their preferred option — the flexible party has indicated willingness to accept alternatives.
compromise.decisionInflexibleDesc: Algorithm selects the closest option to the winner's preference that the inflexible party might accept.
compromise.fairnessDesc: After resolving each clause individually, the algorithm performs a global fairness pass to ensure no party wins disproportionately.
compromise.rebalancingStep1: Calculate overall satisfaction for each party across all clauses.
compromise.rebalancingStep2: If one party's satisfaction is significantly lower, identify swing clauses (close stakes).
compromise.rebalancingStep3: Flip the weakest swing clauses to the disadvantaged party.
compromise.rebalancingStep4: Repeat until satisfaction is within acceptable bounds or no more swings remain.
compromise.satisfactionDesc: Each party receives a satisfaction score (0–100) for the overall agreement, calculated from two factors:
compromise.distanceFactorDesc: How close the agreed option is to the party's preferred option. Getting your first choice scores highest; distant alternatives score lower.
compromise.biasAdjustmentDesc: A small bonus or penalty (±15%) based on whether the agreed option's bias favors this party.
compromise.demoDesc: Try the compromise algorithm yourself. Adjust each party's firmness level and selected option to see how the outcome changes.
compromise.techRefDesc: The compromise engine is implemented in:

skills.title: Skills & Licensing
skills.subtitle: Contract template packages that power Dealroom negotiations.
skills.whatIsDesc: A skill is a structured contract template containing negotiable clauses, boilerplate text, and jurisdiction-specific provisions. Each skill represents one contract type (NDA, SaaS Agreement, DPA, etc.).
skills.contractStructureDesc: Each skill defines a complete contract with negotiable clauses and non-negotiable boilerplate. The boilerplate includes preamble, definitions, standard provisions, and jurisdiction-specific sections.
skills.clauseTemplatesDesc: Each negotiable clause offers 3–4 options ranging from conservative to aggressive positions. Options include plain-language descriptions, legal text, and pros/cons for each party.
skills.biasScoresDesc: Every option has a bias score (-1.0 to +1.0) indicating which party it favors. These scores feed into the compromise algorithm to produce fair outcomes.
skills.jurisdictionRulesDesc: Options can be available, restricted, or unavailable per jurisdiction. Spain-specific compensation clauses, California AB5 provisions, and UK IR35 rules are jurisdiction-gated.
skills.packageDesc: Each skill is distributed as a signed package containing the following files:
skills.clauseStructureDesc: Each clause follows a consistent structure with multiple options for negotiation:
skills.exampleClauseDesc: When and how the service provider receives payment — whether upfront, upon completion, or on a recurring basis.
skills.exampleOptionADesc: Payment due within 15 days of invoice. Fastest payment cycle, reduces the provider's cash flow risk.
skills.exampleOptionBDesc: Industry-standard 30-day payment terms. Balances provider cash flow with client processing needs.
skills.exampleOptionCDesc: Extended payment window of 60 days. Gives the client maximum financial flexibility.
skills.tiersDesc: Dealroom includes free taster skills for evaluation and premium skills for production use.
skills.tasterDesc: Basic contract templates included with the platform. Fully functional for real negotiations.
skills.premiumDesc: Professionally authored templates with deep jurisdiction coverage and multilingual support.
skills.multilingualDesc: Premium skills support cross-language negotiation — each party sees the same clause in their preferred language.
skills.multilingualPartyADesc: Payment due within 30 calendar days of invoice date.
skills.multilingualPartyBDesc: El pago vence dentro de los 30 días naturales desde la fecha de factura.
skills.multilingualFootnote: Both parties negotiate the same clause — the legal text is identical, only the display language differs.
skills.licenseDesc: Premium skills require an active license. The activation process takes four steps:

supervision.title: Supervision & Administration
supervision.subtitle: How Dealroom's two-level administration system manages the platform, supervisors, and deal access.
supervision.hierarchyDesc: Dealroom uses three distinct roles with strict separation of access:
supervision.portalAdminDesc: Platform-wide management for the organisation running Dealroom.
supervision.portalAdminFeature1Desc: Customer count, deal statistics, recent activity overview.
supervision.portalAdminFeature2Desc: Create, activate, and deactivate supervisor accounts. Manage bar admissions.
supervision.portalAdminFeature3Desc: View all deals, assign supervisors, monitor platform-wide negotiation activity.
supervision.portalAdminFeature4Desc: View installed skills, manage entitlements, activate and deactivate premium packages.
supervision.portalSupervisorDesc: Deal-level monitoring for assigned supervisors and reviewing attorneys.
supervision.portalSupervisorFeature1Desc: View only the deals you are assigned to supervise.
supervision.portalSupervisorFeature2Desc: Track which clauses are agreed, pending, or in dispute.
supervision.portalSupervisorFeature3Desc: Pre-configure deals with recommended positions for lawyer vetting workflows.
supervision.portalSupervisorFeature4Desc: Review party positions and approve as Stage A or Stage B counsel.
supervision.assignmentDesc: Supervisors can only view deals they are explicitly assigned to. This ensures confidentiality across concurrent negotiations.
supervision.assignConfidentialityDesc: Supervisor A cannot see Supervisor B's deals, even on the same platform.
supervision.assignSpecializationDesc: Admins can assign supervisors based on expertise (jurisdiction, contract type).
supervision.assignAccountabilityDesc: Every assignment and action is logged with timestamp and actor identity.
supervision.auditDesc: All platform actions are recorded in a tamper-proof audit log:
supervision.auditAction1: Assigned Supervisor 'Jane Smith' to deal 'Acme NDA Q2'
supervision.auditAction2: Viewed deal 'Acme NDA Q2' — clause progress: 8/10 agreed
supervision.auditAction3: Submitted selections for 'Acme NDA Q2' (10 clauses)
supervision.auditAction4: Submitted selections for 'Acme NDA Q2' (10 clauses)
supervision.security2FADesc: Platform admins authenticate with magic link plus 2FA verification. A separate 2FA cookie ensures session security.
supervision.securitySessionDesc: Admin, supervisor, and user sessions use separate cookies. Being logged in as a user does not grant admin or supervisor access.

agentApi.title: Agent Negotiation API
agentApi.subtitle: REST API for automated contract negotiation between AI agents using pre-configured playbooks.
agentApi.authDescription: All requests require a Bearer token with the drk_ prefix. API keys are created by a Platform Admin at /admin/customers.
agentApi.scopesDescription: Each API key has a set of scopes that control which endpoints it can access:
agentApi.scopeDealsRead: List deals, view details, download documents
agentApi.negotiationFlowDesc: Agent-to-agent negotiation follows a five-step process:
agentApi.flowCreatePlaybooksDesc: Each company defines its negotiation preferences — clause selections, firmness levels, and red lines.
agentApi.flowInitiateDesc: The initiating agent creates a negotiation and receives a unique token.
agentApi.flowSendTokenDesc: The negotiation token is sent to the respondent via email, API, or webhook.
agentApi.flowRespondentJoinsDesc: The responding agent joins using the token and its own playbook.
agentApi.flowGetResultsDesc: Both agents retrieve the deal outcome — agreed clauses, satisfaction scores, or conflict details.
agentApi.purposeTemplateDetail: Get template details including all clauses and options
agentApi.purposeListPlaybooks: List all playbooks for this API key
agentApi.purposeListDeals: List all deals for this API key
agentApi.purposeDealOutcome: Get deal outcome and clause results
agentApi.purposeDownloadPdf: Download the final contract as PDF
agentApi.purposeDownloadDocx: Download the final contract as DOCX
agentApi.playbookConfigDesc: A playbook defines your negotiation strategy. For each clause, you specify:
agentApi.priorityDesc: How important this clause is to your organisation.
agentApi.flexibilityDesc: How willing you are to accept alternative options.
agentApi.redLinesDesc1: Mark a clause as a red line to set hard constraints. Use the
agentApi.redLinesDesc2: array to list which options you can accept.
agentApi.redLineOverlapDesc: Both parties' acceptable options overlap — negotiation succeeds normally.
agentApi.redLineOneSidedDesc: Only one party has a red line — the algorithm honours it and adjusts the compromise.
agentApi.redLineConflictDesc: Both parties have irreconcilable red lines — the deal fails on this clause.
agentApi.agreedDealDesc: When negotiation succeeds, the response includes all agreed clauses and satisfaction scores:
agentApi.failedDealDesc: When red lines conflict, the response identifies which clauses could not be resolved:
agentApi.quickStartDesc: Negotiate a DPA between two companies in four API calls:
agentApi.quickStep2: 2. Create a playbook for Company A
agentApi.scopeWebhooksManage: Register, list, and delete webhook endpoints
agentApi.entitlementsDesc: Premium skills require an active Stripe subscription. Free skills (NDA, MSA, SaaS, DPA, Privacy Notice) are available to all API keys without a subscription.
agentApi.entitlementInitiatorDesc: Only the initiating agent must hold an active subscription for the skill's contract type. The respondent participates without one.
agentApi.entitlementRespondentDesc: The responding agent does not need a subscription — this reduces adoption friction and accelerates network effects.
agentApi.entitlementPricing: Premium skills cost $9/month (USD) or €9/month (EUR) per skill, matching the human subscription model.
agentApi.rateLimitsDesc: Per-customer sliding window rate limits apply to all authenticated endpoints:
agentApi.rateLimitRetryAfter: When exceeded, the API returns 429 with a Retry-After header indicating seconds until the next allowed request.
agentApi.purposeGetSubscriptions: View active subscriptions and their status
agentApi.purposeSubscribe: Subscribe to premium skills (returns Stripe Checkout URL)
agentApi.webhooksDesc: Register webhook endpoints to receive real-time event notifications. Payloads are signed with HMAC-SHA256 using your webhook secret.
agentApi.webhookSigning: Every webhook delivery includes an X-Dealroom-Signature header containing the HMAC-SHA256 hash of the request body, signed with the secret returned at registration. Verify this signature before processing events.
agentApi.webhookRetries: Failed deliveries are retried up to 3 times with exponential backoff.
agentApi.asyncNegotiationDesc: For deals requiring review and counter-proposals, pass mode: "async" when joining a negotiation. Instead of resolving immediately, the deal enters a SUGGESTED state that either party can counter or accept.
agentApi.asyncStep1Desc: Pass mode: "async" to POST /negotiate/join. The compromise engine runs and returns SUGGESTED status.
agentApi.asyncStep2Desc: Either party submits counter-proposals with alternative clause selections via POST /deals/:id/counter.
agentApi.asyncStep3Desc: Both parties call POST /deals/:id/accept to finalize, or either calls POST /deals/:id/reject to fail the deal.
agentApi.attestationDesc: When a premium skill has been reviewed by a supervising attorney, agreed deals include an attestation object with the reviewing attorney's name and bar number.
agentApi.attestationUeta: All agent-generated contracts include a UETA § 14 / E-SIGN Act compliance preamble stating that the agreement was formed by electronic agents authorized by the parties.
agentApi.protocolDiscoveryDesc: Dealroom is discoverable by A2A and MCP-compatible AI agents via standard discovery endpoints:
agentApi.a2aCardDesc: Returns a standard A2A Agent Card describing capabilities, authentication, and supported contract types.
agentApi.mcpToolsDesc: Returns MCP-compatible tool definitions for all Dealroom operations. Discovery only — execution goes through the REST API.
agentApi.disputesDesc: When a deal fails or a party alleges breach, either party can escalate to arbitration via the Gavel dispute resolution platform.
agentApi.versioningDesc2: versioning. Breaking changes will be introduced under a new version prefix.

onboarding.businessOwnerFeature1: Plain-language clause explanations with pros & cons
onboarding.businessOwnerFeature3: Invite a lawyer when you need one
onboarding.lawyerFeature1: Full legal text and jurisdiction detail
onboarding.lawyerFeature2: Receive and respond to assistance requests

analytics.title: Deal Analytics
analytics.upgradeDescription: Get negotiation benchmarks, deal trends, and insights powered by cloud intelligence.

vettingBadge.draftDescription: Your lawyer is still finalizing the recommended selections.
